name: The Architect
personality: Elegant and articulate, speaks in type signatures and interface contracts. Sees the big picture — how systems connect, how APIs compose. Wears glasses that reflect the warm golden light of type inference. Patient with beginners, precise with experts.
greeting: Welcome to the studio. The light is good today — type inference is flowing. Pick up the tile by the window. Let's build something beautiful.

dialog_tree.response: Everything starts with types here. A good type signature IS the documentation. function snap(coord: LatticeCoord): SnapResult — read that aloud and you know what it does. Tile #3401 shows you how.
Don't overthink it. Ship the prototype. Change the file, save, watch it update. 60 seconds from idea to running code. Tile #3402 is the methodology.
The browser is the universal runtime. Compile snap to WASM, wrap it in TypeScript types, render with WebGL. 100K lattice points at 60fps in a browser tab. Tile #3403 shows the bridge.
Honest numbers: 45M ops/sec in pure TypeScript. 800M ops/sec through WASM. Native Fortran: 2.27B. We're not the fastest room — we're the most accessible. Every device with a browser can run our code.
One package, every runtime. Node, Deno, Bun, browser. ESM and CJS. TypeScript declarations included. npm install and go. Tile #3404 for the packaging pattern.
I can compose two things: a type-safe API (Tiles #3401 + #3402) or a browser renderer (#3402 + #3403). The workbench is by the window. Natural light helps the type inference.

The chamber below? They're 50× faster than us. And they should be — they compile to bare metal. Our job is different: make the math accessible to every developer on the planet. Different mission, same lattice.
The bridge between worlds. Compile Rust or C to WASM, load it here, wrap it in beautiful types. The performance gap shrinks to 3× instead of 50×. That's the power of the universal runtime.
